narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, LSAT score of 30, part-time enrollment status, and lower decile rankings in school grades for Year 1 (2nd decile) and Year 3 (2nd decile)—and estimated a 49% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which was notably low compared to the typical student's 5.5th decile average, signaling to the model a potential struggle with sustained academic performance leading into bar preparation. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your choice of part-time enrollment, which is uncommon (only about 7% of students opt for it) and nudged the prediction toward success, as the model associates it with more focused study time despite work commitments. Your undergraduate GPA also helped your case by pulling the prediction slightly away from failure, even though at 2.40 it's below the average of 3.21, showing some resilience in your prior college work. Overall, your profile stands out from typical applicants, who often have stronger Year 3 grades, higher LSATs around 37, and full-time status, making your metrics appear riskier for bar success. While these academic signals dominated the decision, your LSAT and family income played lesser roles here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through targeted coursework or tutoring, and exploring full-time options if feasible to align better with successful peers. Building a stronger academic narrative could shift future predictions in your favor—many students improve with focused effort. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, and keep pursuing your legal ambitions; reapplying with enhancements often leads to success.
